questions.stem: A CISSP is asked how to handle the communication with customers during an active incident where details are still unclear. Which approach is BEST?

options.B: Provide timely but accurate communication: acknowledge the situation, specify what is known and what is not, commit to regular updates, identify the single point of contact for customer inquiries, and update the communication as facts develop; avoid both silence (which damages trust) and speculation (which damages credibility)
options.D: Tell customers everything in real time

questions.rationale: Customer communication during active incidents is one of the hardest aspects of incident response because the organization has strong pressures in both directions — legal and strategic pressures to say less, customer and reputation pressures to say more. The mature approach avoids both failure modes: (1) timely acknowledgment that something is happening, so customers know the organization is aware and responding; silence longer than the incident duration damages trust catastrophically; (2) specify what is known and what is not — separate verified facts from unverified reports, separate impact assessments from speculation, separate current state from projected outcomes; (3) commit to regular updates on a defined cadence (hourly, every four hours, daily depending on severity) so customers know when to expect new information; (4) single point of contact for customer inquiries — a customer support channel, a status page, a dedicated email address — so that the organization has consistent messaging; (5) update the communication as facts develop, correcting earlier information when new facts emerge; (6) legal and PR review of customer communication before publication, to ensure consistency and accuracy. Option B captures this balanced approach. Options A and C are opposite failure modes. Option D is too absolute — some information is genuinely not appropriate to share in real time. The CISSP exam tests incident communication because it is a specific, testable discipline and because real incidents are frequently damaged by poor communication choices.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating the operational readiness of a newly-acquired company's security posture. Which assessment is MOST IMPORTANT to conduct FIRST?

options.A: Immediately apply the parent company's policies
options.B: Conduct a risk assessment of the acquired environment including: asset inventory, access to authoritative identity data, known vulnerabilities and unpatched systems, incident history, third-party dependencies, compliance obligations, and any active or recent incidents; use the results to plan integration and remediation priorities without disrupting operations

questions.rationale: Acquisitions inherit both assets and risks. Before integration, the parent company should conduct a thorough security assessment of the acquired environment: (1) asset inventory — what systems exist, where, who owns them; acquisitions frequently have shadow IT and undocumented systems; (2) identity data — who has access to what, including orphaned accounts from the acquired company's own lifecycle gaps; (3) known vulnerabilities and unpatched systems — inherited technical debt; (4) incident history — has the acquired company had security incidents, and are any still active or recent (the Marriott/Starwood 2018 pattern); (5) third-party dependencies — what vendors and sub-processors the acquired company uses, and whether they are acceptable under the parent's standards; (6) compliance obligations — what regulations apply and whether the acquired company is compliant; (7) any active or recent incidents that the parent company would be inheriting. The assessment results feed integration planning: which systems to migrate first, which to decommission, which to leave in place temporarily, and what remediation priorities to schedule. Immediate application of parent policies is inappropriate because it may break business functions the parent does not yet understand, and because the parent's policies may not fit the acquired company's operational reality. Option B captures this. Options A, C, D are wrong. The CISSP exam tests M&A security because it is a common real-world scenario with real consequences.

questions.stem: A CISSP is asked whether to share threat intelligence with industry peers. Which consideration is MOST IMPORTANT?

options.B: Industry threat intelligence sharing (via ISACs, ISAOs, informal peer networks) provides early warning of emerging threats and shared defense against common adversaries, but requires governance: what can be shared (IOCs are usually safe, internal details are not), with whom (vetted members, not public), under what confidentiality terms (TLP traffic light protocol), and with legal review to avoid antitrust or disclosure issues. The right answer is structured sharing within a trust community

questions.rationale: Threat intelligence sharing is a real and valuable practice when done within appropriate governance. Industry ISACs (Information Sharing and Analysis Centers) and ISAOs (Information Sharing and Analysis Organizations) provide structured channels for sharing threat information among trusted peers in the same industry — financial services, healthcare, energy, and others each have established ISACs. Benefits: early warning when an adversary targets one member and is likely to target others, shared defense techniques and playbooks, joint incident response on coordinated attacks, and collective intelligence that individual organizations could not develop alone. Risks without governance: leaking sensitive internal information, antitrust exposure from inappropriate coordination, legal disclosure obligations if shared information is considered to be in the organization's control. The governance elements: (1) what can be shared — IOCs (indicators of compromise) are usually safe, internal operational details, customer information, and business-sensitive data are not; (2) with whom — vetted members of the trust community, not public forums where adversaries can read; (3) under what confidentiality — Traffic Light Protocol (TLP) labels (TLP:RED, TLP:AMBER, TLP:GREEN, TLP:CLEAR) specify how information can be shared and with whom; (4) legal review to avoid antitrust and disclosure issues. Option B captures this. Options A, C, D are wrong. The CISSP exam tests threat sharing because it is current and because the mature answer is structured.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing a physical access control system and finds that cleaning staff have unescorted access to server rooms. Which concern is MOST SIGNIFICANT?

options.B: Unescorted access to sensitive areas violates separation of duties and least privilege; cleaning staff (or anyone outside the server room's authorized access list) should be escorted by authorized personnel, or the cleaning should occur during escorted windows, with logging and supervision. The specific risks include physical tampering, device theft, installation of unauthorized hardware, and cable manipulation

questions.rationale: Unescorted access to sensitive physical areas by personnel whose job function does not require that access is a violation of least privilege applied to physical security. The specific risks are real and documented: (1) physical tampering — disconnecting, moving, or modifying equipment; (2) device theft — removing storage media, drives, or portable devices; (3) installation of unauthorized hardware — keyloggers, rogue network devices, or covert surveillance tools; (4) cable manipulation — disconnecting cables for disruption or tapping traffic; (5) observation of sensitive information — reading screens, documents, or console output; (6) covert photography of the environment. Cleaning staff are not inherently untrustworthy, but unescorted access places them in a position where (a) they could be compelled by external actors, (b) they could be compromised by social engineering, (c) innocent mistakes could cause operational issues, and (d) insider action by a specific individual is unattributable because multiple people have the same access. The mitigation is escorted access (an authorized technical person accompanies the cleaning staff during their time in the server room) or scheduled cleaning windows with supervision, combined with CCTV monitoring, access logging, and clear procedures about what the escort watches for. Option B captures this. Options A, C, D are wrong. The CISSP exam tests physical access because it is a foundational control and because the escort principle is specific and testable.

questions.stem: A CISSP is asked how to maintain evidence integrity for incidents that may result in litigation. Which approach is MOST COMPREHENSIVE?

options.A: Copy files to a USB drive
options.B: Maintain chain of custody from the first response: forensically sound imaging with cryptographic hashes, write blockers for physical media, documentation of every person who handled every piece of evidence with time stamps, tamper-evident storage, eDiscovery preservation per Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (or equivalent), legal counsel engagement early, and retention of evidence per legal hold timelines

questions.rationale: Evidence integrity for litigation requires procedures from the first response that will survive legal scrutiny. The comprehensive approach: (1) chain of custody — documented from the first moment evidence is collected, with every person who handles every piece of evidence recorded with timestamps; (2) forensically sound imaging — bit-for-bit copies with cryptographic hashes (SHA-256 or stronger) computed before and after imaging to prove the image is an exact copy; (3) write blockers — hardware or software that prevents any modification to the original media during imaging, ensuring the original is preserved for potential re-examination; (4) tamper-evident storage — evidence is stored in a way that makes any unauthorized access detectable (sealed envelopes, WORM storage for digital evidence, locked evidence rooms with access logs); (5) eDiscovery preservation — per the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (Rule 37(e)) or equivalent, the organization must preserve electronically stored information relevant to reasonably anticipated litigation; failure to do so can produce sanctions ranging from adverse inference to dismissal of claims; (6) legal counsel engagement early so that attorney-client privilege can cover the investigation and specific legal advice shapes evidence handling; (7) retention of evidence per legal hold timelines — holds override normal retention and continue until litigation concludes. Option B captures this. Options A, C, D are inadequate. The CISSP exam tests evidence handling because it is specific and testable.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing whether to use 'threat hunting' as a proactive capability. Which framing is MOST ACCURATE?

options.A: Threat hunting is unnecessary if you have a SIEM
options.B: Threat hunting is proactive search for indicators of compromise that automated detection has missed. It is most valuable when the organization has sufficient telemetry (logs, EDR, network) and hunters with both technical skill and threat knowledge. Hunting complements automated detection by catching novel or stealthy threats, generating new detection rules from findings, and validating that the detection program is effective. It requires dedicated effort and is not a substitute for automated detection
options.C: Threat hunting is the same as pentesting
options.D: Threat hunting replaces all other operations

questions.rationale: Threat hunting is the proactive search for threats that automated detection has missed. Unlike reactive incident response (which responds to alerts) or automated detection (which catches known patterns), hunting assumes that some compromises go undetected and searches for evidence proactively. The value: (1) catches novel or stealthy threats that do not match existing detection rules; (2) generates new detection rules when findings reveal gaps — a successful hunt that finds a compromise produces signature rules and behavioral rules that become automated detection going forward; (3) validates that the detection program is effective — repeated failed hunts (no findings) suggest either the program is effective or the hunters are not looking in the right places; (4) develops analyst skills and threat knowledge that feed back into the broader SOC. Prerequisites for effective hunting: sufficient telemetry (logs, EDR, network) to search, hunters with both technical skill and threat knowledge (what patterns an adversary would use), hypothesis-driven approach (start with a hypothesis and gather evidence for or against), and dedicated time (hunting squeezed into spare analyst time does not produce results). Hunting complements automated detection — it does not replace SIEM, EDR, or alerting. Option B captures this. Options A, C, D are wrong. The CISSP exam tests threat hunting because it is a specific proactive discipline distinct from reactive SOC work.

questions.stem: A CISSP is designing the vulnerability management lifecycle. Which stage is MOST FREQUENTLY the failure point in real programs?

options.B: Remediation and verification — many programs find vulnerabilities efficiently but fail to close them in operationally meaningful time, lack ownership, produce findings that are never acted on, or close tickets without verifying the fix. The gap between discovery and closed-and-verified remediation is where most real programs fail, and the remediation is governance (clear ownership, SLAs, workflow tracking, escalation, verification)

questions.rationale: Vulnerability management has multiple stages — discovery (scanning, threat intel, vendor advisories), triage (prioritization based on severity and context), remediation (patch, mitigate, compensating control), and verification (confirm the fix is effective) — and the failure point in most real programs is the gap between discovery and verified remediation. Many programs discover vulnerabilities efficiently via scanning and threat intel but fail to close them: (1) no clear ownership — the vulnerability is in an asset that multiple teams share or that no team owns explicitly, so nobody takes responsibility; (2) SLAs that are not enforced — 'critical within 7 days' becomes 'critical within 70 days' because consequences for missing SLAs are absent; (3) workflow that does not track remediation through to completion — tickets are closed when someone plans to patch, not when the patch is verified; (4) no escalation when remediation stalls — aging findings pile up indefinitely; (5) no verification — rescans do not confirm that the reported fix actually resolved the vulnerability. The remediation is governance: clear ownership per asset, enforced SLAs with management visibility for missed deadlines, workflow tracking through to verified closure, escalation when remediation stalls, and mandatory verification before ticket closure. Equifax 2017 is the canonical case study — the critical Apache Struts vulnerability was known to the security team but remediation failed at the governance level. Option B captures this. Options A, C, D are less common failure points.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing the organization's DR strategy. The DR site is in the same metropolitan area as the primary. Which concern is MOST SIGNIFICANT?

options.B: Metropolitan proximity means that both the primary and DR sites could be affected by the same regional disaster (earthquake, hurricane, flood, large-scale power outage, regional network failure, regional terrorism), defeating the purpose of the DR site. The site should be geographically distant enough to be outside the blast radius of plausible regional events — typically hundreds of miles for significant hazards
options.C: DR sites should be next door

questions.rationale: DR site geographic separation is a core consideration because the purpose of the DR site is to survive scenarios that destroy or impair the primary. If both sites are in the same metropolitan area, they share the same regional hazards: (1) earthquakes — the same fault line affects both; (2) hurricanes — the same storm path affects both; (3) flooding — the same watershed or coastal surge affects both; (4) large-scale power outages — the same grid supplies both; (5) regional network failures — the same backbone failures affect both; (6) regional terrorism or civil unrest — the same events affect both. Hurricane Katrina in 2005 taught this lesson painfully: organizations with DR sites in the same coastal region lost both primary and secondary simultaneously. The correct separation depends on the hazards the organization plans for: for significant hazards (hurricanes, earthquakes), separation of hundreds of miles is typical; for more localized events (building fires, localized utility failures), less separation may be acceptable. Many organizations use a two-tier approach: a nearby site for operational recovery from localized events, and a distant site for regional disasters. Option B captures this. Options A, C, D are wrong. The CISSP exam tests DR site selection because it is specific, well-defined, and frequently gets wrong in real-world programs that prioritize operational convenience over disaster planning.

questions.stem: A CISSP is advising on whether to automate the response to every phishing report. Which approach is BEST?

options.B: Automate the high-value, low-risk steps (acknowledge the report, analyze the message for known IOCs, check whether other users received the same message, add IOCs to block lists, scan affected mailboxes); reserve human review for ambiguous cases, high-impact cases (targeting executives or finance), and cases where remediation requires judgment about user communication. The balance is between scale and oversight

questions.rationale: Phishing response is a high-volume activity that benefits from automation, but not all steps are appropriate to fully automate. The value pattern: (1) acknowledge the report — automatable, high value because it confirms to users that their reports are received and encourages more reporting (the leading indicator of phishing program health); (2) analyze the message for known IOCs — automatable, fast and accurate; (3) check whether other users received the same message — automatable, scalable; catches broad campaigns quickly; (4) add IOCs to block lists — automatable for well-established indicators, with human review for novel indicators to avoid false positives; (5) scan affected mailboxes — automatable; (6) reserve human review for ambiguous cases (what does this message mean, is it a legitimate vendor email or a BEC attempt), high-impact cases (targeting executives or finance staff with specific knowledge of ongoing transactions), and cases where remediation requires judgment about user communication (the communication affects employees and must not be heavy-handed). The right balance automates scale work and reserves human attention for judgment work. Option B captures this. Option A (automate everything) may create false positives and user communication problems. Option C (nothing) cannot scale. Option D (only reporting) misses most of the value. The CISSP exam tests automation choices because they are specific and because the mature answer is selective.
